headline: The Sugar Land Skeeters Defend Nothing While North Correa Finally Stops Being the League's Punchline
subheadline: After two straight basement finishes, North Correa swung for the fences with Bobby Witt Jr. at 1.01—and somehow built the most complete roster in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball history.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

Every year, I tell myself I won't care this much about the MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft. And every year, I'm lying to myself like Matt Damon in *The Talented Mr. Ripley*, convincing everyone (mostly me) that this is just a fun little hobby and not a consuming obsession that causes me to ignore my family for three weeks in March. The 2026 draft happened on March 22nd, and I watched all 210 picks unfold in real time like a deranged Brian Koppelman character, taking notes, screaming at my laptop, and texting obscenities to anyone who would listen.

This year's draft had everything: a team coming off back-to-back last-place finishes making a desperate play for relevance, a defending champion sleepwalking through their picks like they'd already mentally moved on to 2027, and at least three picks so baffling that I'm convinced the managers either suffered WiFi outages or were actively trying to sabotage themselves. We had **Shohei Ohtani** going second overall (as a batter only, which... we'll get to that), **Paul Skenes** falling to the 2/3 turn like he'd just been caught in a PED scandal, and someone—SOMEONE—taking **Mike Trout** in the sixth round like it's still 2019 and the man's legs aren't held together by wishes and medical tape.

But here's the thing about snake drafts in 14-team leagues: everyone gets a narrative. The team picking first gets the best player, sure, but they're also waiting 27 picks before they select again. The team picking 14th gets back-to-back selections that can define a season. And somewhere in the middle rounds—always the middle rounds—championships are won and lost. Let's break down who crushed it, who panicked, and who might have just drafted their way out of the league's basement.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The first round unfolded exactly how you'd expect if you've been paying attention to baseball for the last three years. **Bobby Witt Jr.** went 1.01 to **North Correa**, which is the safest, most defensible pick in fantasy baseball right now. The man is a 30-30 shortstop with a .332 average last year, positional eligibility that matters, and the kind of floor that lets you sleep at night. After that? **Shohei Ohtani** (batter only) to the **Athletic Park Dragons** at 1.02, **Mookie Betts** to the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** at 1.03, and **Aaron Judge** to the defending-champion-from-2024 **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** at 1.04.

The real intrigue came at the back of the first round. **Corbin Carroll** at 1.13 to the **Canberra Wombats** feels like a bet on the 2023 version showing up again (the .800 OPS version, not the .685 version from 2024). **Julio Rodríguez** at 1.14 to the **Benchwarmers** is a similar leap of faith—you're drafting the ceiling, not the reality of a guy who's been frustratingly inconsistent for two years. Both picks could look genius by August or disastrous by May. That's the kind of variance that makes the back of the first round feel like a blackjack table at 3 AM.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**NORTH CORREA (Draft Pick #1)**

Finished 14th in 2024. Finished 12th in 2025. That's a franchise that's been wandering through the fantasy wilderness like the Israelites, except instead of 40 years they've only needed two to become a league-wide joke. But here's the thing: this draft might actually fix them. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1.01 is the no-brainer—the guy hit .332 with 32 homers, 26 steals, and a 153 wRC+ last year. He's the best fantasy asset in baseball, full stop. Then they grabbed **Pete Alonso** at 2.28 (who still crushes baseballs despite bouncing to Baltimore), **Logan Gilbert** at 3.29 (a 3.14 ERA, 3.41 FIP workhorse), and **Alex Bregman** at 4.56 for lineup protection. The late-round swing on **Spencer Strider** at 9.113 is high-risk, high-reward—the guy threw 29 innings in 2024 but touched 99 when healthy. **Luis Robert Jr.** at 11.141 and **Dylan Crews** at 12.168 are exactly the kind of upside fliers a basement team should take. This is the most coherent draft I've seen from this franchise.

**Grade: A-** They finally stopped drafting like they were allergic to winning.

**ATHLETIC PARK DRAGONS (Draft Pick #2)**

The Dragons went from 6th place in 2024 to dead last in 2025, which is the kind of collapse that suggests either catastrophic injuries or catastrophic decision-making (probably both). Taking **Shohei Ohtani** at 1.02 as a batter-only makes sense—the man had a 195 wRC+ last year, 54 home runs, and walked more than he struck out. That's generational. But then they went **Matt Olson** at 2.27 (only 26 HR in 2025, 108 wRC+, not exactly vintage Olson) and **Kyle Schwarber** at 3.30. That's a lot of one-dimensional power bats who don't help your average. The pitching is actually solid: **Michael King** at 4.55 was excellent pre-injury, **Shota Imanaga** at 5.58 had a 3.12 ERA as a rookie, and **Aaron Nola** at 6.83 is the definition of reliable. But taking **Ezequiel Tovar** at 10.139 after his 88 wRC+ season feels like a reach—you're paying for the glove, which does nothing in fantasy.

**Grade: B-** Great top of the order, but too much swing-and-miss throughout.

**WISCONSIN RAPIDS REAPERS (Draft Pick #3)**

A brand-new franchise in 2025 that finished 10th—not great, not terrible, the fantasy equivalent of a Coldplay album. **Mookie Betts** at 1.03 is rock solid (143 wRC+, elite OBP, multi-position eligibility), and **Ketel Marte** at 2.26 was a sneaky-great value (146 wRC+ last year, massively underrated). **William Contreras** at 3.31 gives them the best catcher in fantasy. But then things get weird. **Matt McLain** at 4.54 hasn't played a full season yet, **Bryan Reynolds** at 5.59 is fine but uninspiring, and **Christian Yelich** at 6.82 is basically a lottery ticket at this point. The late-round reliever bonanza—**Justin Martinez**, **Jeff Hoffman**, **Jason Foley**, **Jason Adam**—suggests they're punting saves for ratios, which is... a choice.

**Grade: C+** The top is excellent; the middle is concerning; the end is confusing.

**ABBOTSFORD-COLBY TWINS (Draft Pick #4)**

Here's your defending 2024 champion, a team that's finished 8th, 1st, and 6th over the last three years. That's a franchise that knows how to compete. **Aaron Judge** at 1.04 is a no-brainer—the man had 58 home runs and a 221 wRC+ last year, which is legitimately absurd. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** at 2.25 gives them speed and multi-position flexibility. **Austin Riley** at 3.32 is boring but good (35 HR, 123 wRC+). The pitching staff of **Pablo López**, **Spencer Schwellenbach**, and **Zac Gallen** is deep if not spectacular. But here's my concern: **Brice Turang** at 8.109 and **Xavier Edwards** at 9.116 are both speed-and-defense guys who don't hit enough for fantasy relevance. This feels like a team that's trying to get too cute.

**Grade: B** Champions don't rebuild; they reload. This was more of a pivot.

**BASEBALLPRO77 (Draft Pick #5)**

The only two-time finalist in league history (champion in 2023, runner-up in 2024) crashed to 7th last year, which is basically the *Succession* finale of fantasy trajectories—eventually, even the great ones fall. **Elly De La Cruz** at 1.05 is the most exciting pick in the draft: a 6'5" shortstop who hit 25 homers and stole 67 bases last year. SIXTY-SEVEN. That's not a stat, that's a constitutional amendment. **Jackson Merrill** at 2.24 was ROY-worthy in 2025 (22 HR, .292 AVG, 129 wRC+), and **Cole Ragans** at 3.33 was one of the best pitchers in the AL (2.77 ERA, 3.15 FIP, 225 Ks). But taking **Emmanuel Clase** at 4.52 is aggressive for a closer—even the best one in baseball. The late-round value is insane: **Luis Arraez** at 10.136 (career .315 hitter), **Bo Bichette** at 11.145 (injury bounceback candidate), **Royce Lewis** at 12.164 (30-HR upside when healthy).

**Grade: A-** They're one Elly breakout from running it back.

**ALBUQUERQUE BOMBERS (Draft Pick #6)**

Finished 5th, 10th, and 3rd over the last three years—a franchise that's always competitive but never quite gets there, like the Clippers or my dating life in my twenties. **José Ramírez** at 1.06 is the safest pick possible: 39 HR, 142 wRC+, .273 AVG, and he's never injured. **Freddie Freeman** at 2.23 had a down year in 2025 (117 wRC+) but is still Freddie Freeman. **Garrett Crochet** at 3.34 is fascinating—the guy dominated in his first full season (3.10 ERA, elite K rate) but has an innings limit history. **Teoscar Hernández** at 4.51 and **Logan Webb** at 5.62 are both solid, and getting **Willy Adames** at 6.79 after he signed that mega-deal with the Giants is smart. This is a workmanlike draft—no fireworks, but no disasters.

**Grade: B+** They're going to be competitive again. They're also probably going to finish third again.

**HIROSHIMA RADIATION (Draft Pick #7)**

A team that finished 2nd in 2023, then cratered to 11th, then rebounded to 5th. The volatility is alarming, like watching someone play fantasy baseball while also playing Russian roulette. **Juan Soto** at 1.07 is elite (196 wRC+, .419 OBP, generational eye), but taking **Trea Turner** at 2.22 after his injury-plagued 2025 (104 wRC+, only 12 SB) is concerning. **Blake Snell** at 3.35 is a bet on his 2023 Cy Young form returning, not his 2024 disaster. **Jose Altuve** at 4.50 is 35 years old. **Tyler Glasnow** at 5.63 has never thrown 200 innings in his life. This entire draft is a bet on players being their best versions, which is either brilliant or delusional.

**Grade: B-** High ceiling, but the floor is terrifying.

**WONDER WHARF WONDERDOGS (Draft Pick #8)**

Finished 4th, 5th, and 2nd over the last three years without ever winning it all—the Buffalo Bills of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at 1.08 had a monster 2025 (157 wRC+, 35 HR, .313 AVG), and with that new 1B/3B eligibility, he's more valuable than ever. **Bryce Harper** at 2.21 is still Bryce Harper (141 wRC+, .987 OPS in 2025). **Corbin Burnes** at 3.36 anchors the rotation. But here's the interesting move: **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at 5.64. That's 64th overall for a guy who was the consensus 1.01 two years ago. He's coming off a torn ACL in 2024 and only played 52 games in 2025, but the upside is still 40-70 with a .900+ OPS. If he's healthy? This team wins the league.

**Grade: A-** The Acuña pick is either the steal of the draft or a waste of a roster spot. No in-between.

**BUTTVILLE SKIDMARKS (Draft Pick #9)**

First of all, incredible team name. (This is the kind of thing that separates good leagues from great ones.) Finished 11th, 8th, and 8th over the last three years—the definition of mediocrity. **Kyle Tucker** at 1.09 is excellent (32 HR, 135 wRC+, 23 SB), and **Zack Wheeler** at 2.20 was one of the best pitchers in baseball (2.57 ERA, 3.01 FIP). **Rafael Devers** at 3.37 is a batting title contender (.303 AVG last year). But then they took **Jacob deGrom** at 5.65, and I need to talk about this. deGrom threw 30 innings in 2025. He's 38 years old. His elbow has more procedures than a hospital wing. This is hope masquerading as strategy. Getting **J.T. Realmuto** at 12.160 is nice value for a catcher, though.

**Grade: B** Solid foundation, but the deGrom pick could torpedo them.

**GENERAL HOSPITAL (Draft Pick #10)**

Back-to-back 13th-place finishes. This is a franchise in crisis, and the draft name is either ironic or prophetic. **Francisco Lindor** at 1.10 is great (28 HR, 126 wRC+, still a top-5 SS), and **Jarren Duran** at 2.19 was the breakout of 2024-25 (24 HR, 34 SB, .285 AVG). **Chris Sale** at 3.38 won the Cy Young last year at age 36, proving that necromancy is real. But taking **Mike Trout** at 6.75 is... look, I understand the allure. I do. But the man has played 110 games over the last three years combined. He's 34. His body has betrayed him in ways that would make a medieval torture victim wince. This is a reach that could define their season.

**Grade: C** The Trout pick is a disaster waiting to happen.

**SUGAR LAND SKEETERS (Draft Pick #11)**

Your 2025 defending champions, baby! Finished 13th, 3rd, and 1st over the last three years—a true Cinderella story. But this draft has me worried. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 1.11 is still electric (31 HR, 24 SB) but can't stay healthy. **Tarik Skubal** at 2.18 was the best pitcher in baseball last year (2.39 ERA, 2.30 FIP, Cy Young winner), so that's a slam dunk. **Cody Bellinger** at 3.39 after his Yankees resurgence is solid. But **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** at 4.46 missed significant time in 2025 with shoulder issues. **Josh Hader** at 5.69 is 31 and his velocity dipped last year. This feels like a champion resting on their laurels.

**Grade: B-** Defending champions shouldn't coast, and this feels like coasting.

**RIVERSIDE ROUGHNECKS (Draft Pick #12)**

Three straight finishes of 9th, 9th, and 11th. That's aggressively mediocre. But this draft might change that. **Jackson Chourio** at 1.12 was outstanding as a 20-year-old (21 HR, 22 SB, .271 AVG), and the arrow is pointing straight up. Then they got **Gunnar Henderson** at 2.17. GUNNAR HENDERSON. The guy with 37 HR, 127 wRC+, and elite shortstop eligibility fell to the 2/3 turn. That's borderline criminal. **Ozzie Albies** at 3.40 and **Manny Machado** at 4.45 give them a rock-solid infield. **Roki Sasaki** at 7.96 is the wildcard—if the Dodgers bring him along slowly, he could be a Cy Young candidate by year-end. Or he could get hurt. There is no in-between with Japanese pitchers anymore.

**Grade: A** The Henderson-Chourio pairing could anchor a dynasty.

**CANBERRA WOMBATS (Draft Pick #13)**

Finished 3rd in 2023, cratered to 12th in 2024, bounced back to 4th in 2025. Volatile! **Corbin Carroll** at 1.13 is a bet on the 2023 version (.868 OPS, 25 HR, 54 SB) and not the 2024 version (.685 OPS, disappointment incarnate). He rebounded somewhat in 2025, but this is still a risk. **Yordan Alvarez** at 2.16 is a monster (156 wRC+, 35 HR) but comes with DH-only limitations. **Wyatt Langford** at 3.41 had an up-and-down rookie year. **Josh Hader** at 5.69—wait, they didn't get Hader, that was Sugar Land. Sorry, **Josh Hader** wasn't available. They got... *checks notes* ...also **Josh Hader** at 5.69. No, wait. *Actually checks notes*. They got **Cal Raleigh** at 6.72, which is excellent catcher value. The **Shane McClanahan** at 13.181 pickup could be a league-winner if his shoulder cooperates.

**Grade: B** Boom-or-bust roster that'll either finish 3rd or 10th.

**BENCHWARMERS (Draft Pick #14)**

Finished 14th in 2023, rose to 7th in 2024, settled back to 9th in 2025. That's progress! **Julio Rodríguez** at 1.14 is the most frustrating pick because you're drafting what he COULD be, not what he's been (112 wRC+ last year, only 20 HR, the power regression is real). But **Paul Skenes** at 2.15 falling to them is ABSURD. The guy had a 1.96 ERA in 23 starts as a rookie. He's unhittable. He's the best young pitcher in baseball. And they got him AND their first-round pick back-to-back. **Oneil Cruz** at 3.42 is all upside (21 HR, 24 SB, but also 173 Ks), and **Dylan Cease** at 4.43 is underrated (3.36 ERA, 224 Ks). The **Adley Rutschman** pick at 6.71 gives them a catcher who gets on base, and **Tanner Bibee** at 8.99 was excellent in 2025.

**Grade: A-** The Skenes gift alone makes this draft a success.

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE THREE BEST VALUE PICKS:**

1. **Gunnar Henderson, Rd 2, Pick 17 to Riverside Roughnecks** — Henderson was a top-10 player in fantasy last year (37 HR, 6.6 WAR, 127 wRC+). Getting him at pick 17 is like finding a Van Gogh at a garage sale. Someone fell asleep at the wheel.

2. **Paul Skenes, Rd 2, Pick 15 to Benchwarmers** — A 1.96 ERA as a rookie! He struck out 170 in 133 innings! He fell to the 2/3 turn because... pitchers are scary? I have no explanation.

3. **Ronald Acuña Jr., Rd 5, Pick 64 to Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Yes, he's coming off injuries. Yes, he only played 52 games last year. But pick 64 for a guy who was the consensus 1.01 in 2024? If he plays 120+ games, this is the steal of the decade.

**THE THREE WORST REACHES:**

1. **Mike Trout, Rd 6, Pick 75 to General Hospital** — He's played 110 games in three years. He's 34. His body is actively rebelling against the concept of professional athletics. This is nostalgia, not strategy.

2. **Jacob deGrom, Rd 5, Pick 65 to Buttville Skidmarks** — See above, but worse. deGrom is 38, has thrown 59 innings over the last two seasons combined, and his elbow should be in a medical museum.

3. **Ezequiel Tovar, Rd 10, Pick 139 to Athletic Park Dragons** — An 88 wRC+ shortstop whose value is entirely tied to real-life defense, which does nothing in fantasy. This pick made no sense.

**V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **North Correa finishes in the top 3.** After two straight basement finishes, they finally got the 1.01 right AND surrounded **Bobby Witt Jr.** with legitimate talent. The **Strider** and **Robert Jr.** fliers will pay off.

2. **Sugar Land Skeeters miss the playoffs.** The defending champions drafted like a team that was satisfied with last year's trophy. **Tatis** and **Yamamoto** will both miss significant time. The empire crumbles.

3. **Riverside Roughnecks win the league.** The **Henderson**-**Chourio** pairing is the best 1-2 punch in the league, and **Roki Sasaki** becomes an ace by August.

4. **General Hospital finishes last again.** The **Trout** pick will backfire spectacularly. He'll play 40 games. They'll be stuck chasing points all year.

5. **Ronald Acuña Jr. is a top-5 fantasy player.** The Wonderdogs get the 2023 version back, and that fifth-round pick looks like highway robbery by season's end.

**VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

I'm going with the **Riverside Roughnecks**.

Here's why: they got **Gunnar Henderson** at pick 17 and **Jackson Chourio** at pick 12. That's two top-15 talents who are both under 22 years old and ascending. **Ozzie Albies** provides category balance, **Manny Machado** is underrated stability, and **Roki Sasaki** has the highest ceiling of any pitcher in this draft. They've been aggressively mediocre for three straight years (9th, 9th, 11th), which means they were due for a breakout. This is the draft that changes their trajectory.

The Wonderdogs have the upside if Acuña returns to form. BaseballPro77 has the track record and **Elly De La Cruz**. North Correa has the best player in fantasy. But the Roughnecks have the combination of youth, value, and ceiling that wins leagues.

In a 14-team league with this much parity, you need to steal picks in rounds 2-5 to separate yourself. The Roughnecks didn't just steal picks—they committed grand larceny. **Henderson** falling to them is the kind of mistake that haunts a league for years.

This is their year. And if I'm wrong? At least I didn't draft **Mike Trout** in the sixth round.

*See you in October.*
